dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 78 responding for a seizure.
Hi, my name is Marcus, and I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
My husband was having a seizure, but it stopped about 5 minutes ago. He's still a bit out of it.
Can you tell me your name?
And tell me where we are right now?
Clench and relax your right hand for me.
Eye, verbal, and motor responses all appropriate. GCS is 15 with full orientation.
Airway is intact with no signs of obstruction.
Holding his pulse, it's around 102 beats per minute, strong.
His respiratory rate is 12 breaths per minute, consistent and unlabored.
Blood pressure is 152 over 90.
EKG is showing sinus rhythm with signs of sinus tachycardia.
Oxygen saturation is 95%, which seems normal.
Breathing is unlabored without signs of distress.
His skin is warm and sweaty, pulse is strong and elevated.
First reading shows 12 breaths per minute, stable.
Does he have any known medical conditions or seizure history?
I was upstairs and didn't see the seizure. When I came down, he was on the floor in a postictal state.
Let me attempt an intravenous line for possible medication or fluids.
What medications is he currently taking?
He has a history of seizures, last one was about two years ago.
What was the last time he had something to eat or drink?
Skin is warm, intact, and there are no signs of trauma.
How would you rate the worst pain you had during the seizure on a scale from 0 to 10?
Skin remains normal with no new changes. No petechiae or signs of trauma observed.
IV access has been established in the left arm for monitoring and fluid administration.
We are applying a 4-lead ECG to monitor cardiac rhythm.
Blood glucose is 240 mg/dL, consistent with hyperglycemia.
Lung and heart assessment will be deferred unless clinical indication arises.
Head and neck are visually normal with no signs of injury.
He didn't seem to have a specific trigger from what I saw.
He's on amlodipine (Lotrel) for blood pressure and lamotrigine (Lamictal) for seizures.
Second pulse reading is 105-still elevated, but no change in rhythm.
His respiration rate is now at 11 breaths per minute.
Blood pressure is now 148 over 97.
Cardiac rhythm remains as normal sinus rhythm.
His oxygen saturation is down to 94%, but still within acceptable ranges.
He was taking his usual medications recently.
He wasn't showing any unusual symptoms before the seizure started.
We will continue monitoring for any signs of another seizure or neurological decline.
We are transporting him to [COMPANY_NAME] for further neurological assessment.
Patient is secured using the five-point harness for transport safety.
The IV site is masked and taped to prevent dislodgement during transport.
Handing off to ED staff at [COMPANY_NAME] Bed 14. Report and monitoring data ready.
Transport initiated to [COMPANY_NAME] on schedule and within protocol.
His awareness is increasing during transport and he is even starting to talk more.
Vital signs indicate that the patient is stable with no recent changes.
No pain reported on the pain scale and his respiratory effort is consistent.
All external restraints and ambulance straps are secure.
He mentioned previously using marijuana this morning, which ED will confirm.
Routine monitoring was applied during transport with IV access and 4-lead ECG.
No movement in transport. All parts fully strapped and bed secured.
The patient appears to be regaining normal cognitive function as he becomes more alert.
Vital checks show no major changes from arrival.
Core signs remain the same-no additional seizures or neurological changes.
All steps in protocol 3-12 for seizures have been completed appropriately.
He consumed marijuana earlier this morning, per his admission.
No new signs of injury or abrasion added to the skin.
Current medications are confirmed and in line with ePCR documentation.
The patient appears to be regaining normal cognitive function as he becomes more alert.
Vital checks show no major changes from arrival.
[COMPANY_NAME] at Bed 14 is the destination. Paperwork and outlines for transfer are prepared.
All protocol steps have been carried out as required for this medical-seizure case.
